meta.title: How Americans Wanted the U.S. to Handle Iran-On the Eve of the Attack

question.title: Survey Question
question.prompt: Thinking about the current situation with Iran, which comes closest to your view on U.S. policy?

questionResults.label: The U.S. should use military force if necessary
The U.S. should use diplomacy and sanctions
The U.S. should take a less involved role in the situation with Iran

key_takeaways: 58.9% of Americans opposed using military force.
The partisan divide is 14.8 pp, but even among Republicans only 32.5% supported military action.
Men were more supportive of military action than women. The gender divide was 8.3 pp overall and 11.5 among Republicans.
Rural voters were 7 percentage points more supportive of strikes than urban voters.
Age differences are modest overall, though Baby Boomer Democrats were 3 pp more opposed to strikes than Gen Z Democrats.
Education, income, and religion had little independent effect once other demographic and partisan differences are accounted for.
African Americans were 12.4 pp less supportive of strikes than White Americans and 6.9 pp less than Hispanics.
Disaffected Young Men and the White Working-Class, where 10-10.7 pp less in favor of military intervention than Evangelical Republicans.
content.methodology_note: We estimate a machine learning ensemble that predicts opinion as a function of party, geography, age, sex, education, income, religion, race, and contextual variables. We generate predicted probabilities for detailed demographic cells and post-stratify them to the county, congressional district, state house, and state senate levels using turnout-adjusted population weights before aggregating to the national level. Reported differences (e.g., rural vs. urban) reflect differences in predicted support for those populations as they are composed—including their partisan and demographic makeup—rather than simple unweighted survey averages. As a result, rural–urban differences are not strictly ceteris paribus; they partly reflect differences in partisan composition. For ceteris paribus-style comparisons, we report within-group breakdowns and crosstabs (e.g., rural vs. urban within party).
